guest: Anand (Formidable Fellowship founder) + 3 student entrepreneurs: Lincoln Snyder, I on, Abigail

business_ideas.idea: Sunshine Exteriors: window cleaning, gutters, pressure washing, fence staining, holiday lighting
Teens to Table: homemade baked goods wholesale to establishments and school suppliers
Growing and selling chrysanthemums (mums) to wholesalers and retail customers
Formidable Fellowship: nonprofit giving grants to young entrepreneurs to build businesses

predictions.text: Will grow revenue to $150K this year from $60K last year
Will grow cookies business 600% and reach $25K revenue this year
Will double mums revenue to $30K this year
Lincoln could 4x his business in one year if he replicates across neighborhoods
Lincoln will be a millionaire within ~2 years if he continues at current trajectory
Abigail's cold-email strategy will get her meetings with major retailers within 30 days

dynamics: Sam provides hype, storytelling, and big-picture encouragement. Shaan grounds conversations with data, frameworks, and tactical advice. Anand presents the three student founders for 'office hours' style Q&A sessions where each student pitches a problem and gets mentorship. High energy, supportive tone with emphasis on validating young entrepreneurs' accomplishments.
frameworks: Rule of Reciprocity: doing something nice to someone makes them reciprocate, often disproportionately
Pond businesses: if a business model works in one neighborhood/pond, it replicates across similar ponds
The 3 ways to grow: sell more, get customers to buy more/more often, or raise prices
Gap year pitch: ask parents for 1-year trial, not lifetime exemption from college
Cold email persistence: most people don't try; those who do get meetings with CEOs
Your story is your most valuable asset in early business
learnings: Young entrepreneurs' biggest advantage is their naivety—they don't overthink or second-guess execution
A business model that works in one neighborhood can be scaled across dozens of similar neighborhoods at minimal additional cost
Raising prices on existing customers often works better than building complex paid acquisition strategies
The window to use your 'young founder' advantage is limited—2-6 years—use it while being cute/compelling still resonates
Most people lose because they never try to reach the people they think are unreachable; those who do succeed
Immediate profits matter less than trajectory and story for long-term value creation

banger_quotes.quote: If you do roughly 300 grand of seller earnings you are worth a million dollar business.
Most people don't even try. Anybody that tried reached somebody.
You are literally one email and 20 follow-ups away from just about everyone on earth.
You're going to make more money than your professors.
Sell to the people who have money.

numbers_dropped: Lincoln's Sunshine Exteriors: $60K revenue year 1, 50% margin = $30K profit, projecting $150K year 2
Lincoln's Nextdoor ads: $9 cost per lead, $30 CAC, $600-650 average transaction value, 21:1 ROAS, 90 customers
I on's Teens to Table: $4K revenue year 1, projecting $25K year 2 (600% growth)
I on's cookie costs: 27-28 cents to make, selling wholesale at 40-80 cents
I on's margins: $20 profit per 700-cookie order with current pricing
Abigail's mums business: $15K revenue year 1, $7-8K profit, projecting $30K revenue year 2
Anand's Formidable Fellowship: $500K initial + $700K donations = $1.2M to give $1K grants to 23+ young entrepreneurs
notable: Unusual format: office-hours style with three student entrepreneurs simultaneously; Anand Sinha from Formidable Fellowship is the featured partner-guest running the program; emphasis on validating that young people can build real revenue-generating businesses while in high school.
